PART 1




CHAPTER ONE
PLAYING PILGRIMS


芒聙聹Christmas won芒聙聶t be Christmas without any presents,芒聙聺 grumbled Jo, lying
on the rug.

芒聙聹It芒聙聶s so dreadful to be poor!芒聙聺 sighed Meg, looking down at her old
dress.

芒聙聹I don芒聙聶t think it芒聙聶s fair for some girls to have plenty of pretty
things, and other girls nothing at all,芒聙聺 added little Amy, with an
injured sniff.

芒聙聹We芒聙聶ve got Father and Mother, and each other,芒聙聺 said Beth contentedly
from her corner.

The four young faces on which the firelight shone brightened at the
cheerful words, but darkened again as Jo said sadly, 芒聙聹We haven芒聙聶t got
Father, and shall not have him for a long time.芒聙聺 She didn芒聙聶t say
芒聙聹perhaps never,芒聙聺 but each silently added it, thinking of Father far
away, where the fighting was.

Nobody spoke for a minute; then Meg said in an altered tone, 芒聙聹You know
the reason Mother proposed not having any presents this Christmas was
because it is going to be a hard winter for everyone; and she thinks we
ought not to spend money for pleasure, when our men are suffering so in
the army. We can芒聙聶t do much, but we can make our little sacrifices, and
ought to do it gladly. But I am afraid I don芒聙聶t,芒聙聺 and Meg shook her
head, as she thought regretfully of all the pretty things she wanted.

芒聙聹But I don芒聙聶t think the little we should spend would do any good. We芒聙聶ve
each got a dollar, and the army wouldn芒聙聶t be much helped by our giving
that. I agree not to expect anything from Mother or you, but I do want
to buy _Undine and Sintran_ for myself. I芒聙聶ve wanted it so long,芒聙聺 said
Jo, who was a bookworm.

芒聙聹I planned to spend mine in new music,芒聙聺 said Beth, with a little sigh,
which no one heard but the hearth brush and kettle-holder.

芒聙聹I shall get a nice box of Faber芒聙聶s drawing pencils; I really need
them,芒聙聺 said Amy decidedly.

芒聙聹Mother didn芒聙聶t say anything about our money, and she won芒聙聶t wish us to
give up everything. Let芒聙聶s each buy what we want, and have a little fun;
I芒聙聶m sure we work hard enough to earn it,芒聙聺 cried Jo, examining the heels
of her shoes in a gentlemanly manner.

芒聙聹I know I do芒聙聰teaching those tiresome children nearly all day, when I芒聙聶m
longing to enjoy myself at home,芒聙聺 began Meg, in the complaining tone
again.

芒聙聹You don芒聙聶t have half such a hard time as I do,芒聙聺 said Jo. 芒聙聹How would you
like to be shut up for hours with a nervous, fussy old lady, who keeps
you trotting, is never satisfied, and worries you till you芒聙聶re ready to
fly out the window or cry?芒聙聺

芒聙聹It芒聙聶s naughty to fret, but I do think washing dishes and keeping things
tidy is the worst work in the world. It makes me cross, and my hands
get so stiff, I can芒聙聶t practice well at all.芒聙聺 And Beth looked at her
rough hands with a sigh that any one could hear that time.

芒聙聹I don芒聙聶t believe any of you suffer as I do,芒聙聺 cried Amy, 芒聙聹for you don芒聙聶t
have to go to school with impertinent girls, who plague you if you
don芒聙聶t know your lessons, and laugh at your dresses, and label your
father if he isn芒聙聶t rich, and insult you when your nose isn芒聙聶t nice.芒聙聺

芒聙聹If you mean libel, I芒聙聶d say so, and not talk about labels, as if Papa
was a pickle bottle,芒聙聺 advised Jo, laughing.

芒聙聹I know what I mean, and you needn芒聙聶t be statirical about it. It芒聙聶s
proper to use good words, and improve your vocabilary,芒聙聺 returned Amy,
with dignity.

芒聙聹Don芒聙聶t peck at one another, children. Don芒聙聶t you wish we had the money
Papa lost when we were little, Jo? Dear me! How happy and good we芒聙聶d be,
if we had no worries!芒聙聺 said Meg, who could remember better times.

芒聙聹You said the other day you thought we were a deal happier than the
King children, for they were fighting and fretting all the time, in
spite of their money.芒聙聺

芒聙聹So I did, Beth. Well, I think we are. For though we do have to work,
we make fun of ourselves, and are a pretty jolly set, as Jo would say.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Jo does use such slang words!芒聙聺 observed Amy, with a reproving look at
the long figure stretched on the rug.

Jo immediately sat up, put her hands in her pockets, and began to
whistle.

芒聙聹Don芒聙聶t, Jo. It芒聙聶s so boyish!芒聙聺

芒聙聹That芒聙聶s why I do it.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I detest rude, unladylike girls!芒聙聺

芒聙聹I hate affected, niminy-piminy chits!芒聙聺

芒聙聹Birds in their little nests agree,芒聙聺 sang Beth, the peacemaker, with
such a funny face that both sharp voices softened to a laugh, and the
芒聙聹pecking芒聙聺 ended for that time.

芒聙聹Really, girls, you are both to be blamed,芒聙聺 said Meg, beginning to
lecture in her elder-sisterly fashion. 芒聙聹You are old enough to leave off
boyish tricks, and to behave better, Josephine. It didn芒聙聶t matter so
much when you were a little girl, but now you are so tall, and turn up
your hair, you should remember that you are a young lady.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I芒聙聶m not! And if turning up my hair makes me one, I芒聙聶ll wear it in two
tails till I芒聙聶m twenty,芒聙聺 cried Jo, pulling off her net, and shaking down
a chestnut mane. 芒聙聹I hate to think I芒聙聶ve got to grow up, and be Miss
March, and wear long gowns, and look as prim as a China Aster! It芒聙聶s bad
enough to be a girl, anyway, when I like boy芒聙聶s games and work and
manners! I can芒聙聶t get over my disappointment in not being a boy. And
it芒聙聶s worse than ever now, for I芒聙聶m dying to go and fight with Papa. And
I can only stay home and knit, like a poky old woman!芒聙聺

And Jo shook the blue army sock till the needles rattled like
castanets, and her ball bounded across the room.

芒聙聹Poor Jo! It芒聙聶s too bad, but it can芒聙聶t be helped. So you must try to be
contented with making your name boyish, and playing brother to us
girls,芒聙聺 said Beth, stroking the rough head with a hand that all the
dish washing and dusting in the world could not make ungentle in its
touch.

芒聙聹As for you, Amy,芒聙聺 continued Meg, 芒聙聹you are altogether too particular
and prim. Your airs are funny now, but you芒聙聶ll grow up an affected
little goose, if you don芒聙聶t take care. I like your nice manners and
refined ways of speaking, when you don芒聙聶t try to be elegant. But your
absurd words are as bad as Jo芒聙聶s slang.芒聙聺

芒聙聹If Jo is a tomboy and Amy a goose, what am I, please?芒聙聺 asked Beth,
ready to share the lecture.

芒聙聹You芒聙聶re a dear, and nothing else,芒聙聺 answered Meg warmly, and no one
contradicted her, for the 芒聙聵Mouse芒聙聶 was the pet of the family.

As young readers like to know 芒聙聵how people look芒聙聶, we will take this
moment to give them a little sketch of the four sisters, who sat
knitting away in the twilight, while the December snow fell quietly
without, and the fire crackled cheerfully within. It was a comfortable
room, though the carpet was faded and the furniture very plain, for a
good picture or two hung on the walls, books filled the recesses,
chrysanthemums and Christmas roses bloomed in the windows, and a
pleasant atmosphere of home peace pervaded it.

Margaret, the eldest of the four, was sixteen, and very pretty, being
plump and fair, with large eyes, plenty of soft brown hair, a sweet
mouth, and white hands, of which she was rather vain. Fifteen-year-old
Jo was very tall, thin, and brown, and reminded one of a colt, for she
never seemed to know what to do with her long limbs, which were very
much in her way. She had a decided mouth, a comical nose, and sharp,
gray eyes, which appeared to see everything, and were by turns fierce,
funny, or thoughtful. Her long, thick hair was her one beauty, but it
was usually bundled into a net, to be out of her way. Round shoulders
had Jo, big hands and feet, a flyaway look to her clothes, and the
uncomfortable appearance of a girl who was rapidly shooting up into a
woman and didn芒聙聶t like it. Elizabeth, or Beth, as everyone called her,
was a rosy, smooth-haired, bright-eyed girl of thirteen, with a shy
manner, a timid voice, and a peaceful expression which was seldom
disturbed. Her father called her 芒聙聵Little Miss Tranquility芒聙聶, and the
name suited her excellently, for she seemed to live in a happy world of
her own, only venturing out to meet the few whom she trusted and loved.
Amy, though the youngest, was a most important person, in her own
opinion at least. A regular snow maiden, with blue eyes, and yellow
hair curling on her shoulders, pale and slender, and always carrying
herself like a young lady mindful of her manners. What the characters
of the four sisters were we will leave to be found out.

The clock struck six and, having swept up the hearth, Beth put a pair
of slippers down to warm. Somehow the sight of the old shoes had a good
effect upon the girls, for Mother was coming, and everyone brightened
to welcome her. Meg stopped lecturing, and lighted the lamp, Amy got
out of the easy chair without being asked, and Jo forgot how tired she
was as she sat up to hold the slippers nearer to the blaze.

芒聙聹They are quite worn out. Marmee must have a new pair.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I thought I芒聙聶d get her some with my dollar,芒聙聺 said Beth.

芒聙聹No, I shall!芒聙聺 cried Amy.

芒聙聹I芒聙聶m the oldest,芒聙聺 began Meg, but Jo cut in with a decided, 芒聙聹I芒聙聶m the man
of the family now Papa is away, and I shall provide the slippers, for
he told me to take special care of Mother while he was gone.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I芒聙聶ll tell you what we芒聙聶ll do,芒聙聺 said Beth, 芒聙聹let芒聙聶s each get her something
for Christmas, and not get anything for ourselves.芒聙聺

芒聙聹That芒聙聶s like you, dear! What will we get?芒聙聺 exclaimed Jo.

Everyone thought soberly for a minute, then Meg announced, as if the
idea was suggested by the sight of her own pretty hands, 芒聙聹I shall give
her a nice pair of gloves.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Army shoes, best to be had,芒聙聺 cried Jo.

芒聙聹Some handkerchiefs, all hemmed,芒聙聺 said Beth.

芒聙聹I芒聙聶ll get a little bottle of cologne. She likes it, and it won芒聙聶t cost
much, so I芒聙聶ll have some left to buy my pencils,芒聙聺 added Amy.

芒聙聹How will we give the things?芒聙聺 asked Meg.

芒聙聹Put them on the table, and bring her in and see her open the bundles.
Don芒聙聶t you remember how we used to do on our birthdays?芒聙聺 answered Jo.

芒聙聹I used to be so frightened when it was my turn to sit in the chair
with the crown on, and see you all come marching round to give the
presents, with a kiss. I liked the things and the kisses, but it was
dreadful to have you sit looking at me while I opened the bundles,芒聙聺
said Beth, who was toasting her face and the bread for tea at the same
time.

芒聙聹Let Marmee think we are getting things for ourselves, and then
surprise her. We must go shopping tomorrow afternoon, Meg. There is so
much to do about the play for Christmas night,芒聙聺 said Jo, marching up
and down, with her hands behind her back, and her nose in the air.

芒聙聹I don芒聙聶t mean to act any more after this time. I芒聙聶m getting too old for
such things,芒聙聺 observed Meg, who was as much a child as ever about
芒聙聵dressing-up芒聙聶 frolics.

芒聙聹You won芒聙聶t stop, I know, as long as you can trail round in a white gown
with your hair down, and wear gold-paper jewelry. You are the best
actress we芒聙聶ve got, and there芒聙聶ll be an end of everything if you quit the
boards,芒聙聺 said Jo. 芒聙聹We ought to rehearse tonight. Come here, Amy, and do
the fainting scene, for you are as stiff as a poker in that.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I can芒聙聶t help it. I never saw anyone faint, and I don芒聙聶t choose to make
myself all black and blue, tumbling flat as you do. If I can go down
easily, I芒聙聶ll drop. If I can芒聙聶t, I shall fall into a chair and be
graceful. I don芒聙聶t care if Hugo does come at me with a pistol,芒聙聺 returned
Amy, who was not gifted with dramatic power, but was chosen because she
was small enough to be borne out shrieking by the villain of the piece.

芒聙聹Do it this way. Clasp your hands so, and stagger across the room,
crying frantically, 芒聙聵Roderigo! Save me! Save me!芒聙聶芒聙聺 and away went Jo,
with a melodramatic scream which was truly thrilling.

Amy followed, but she poked her hands out stiffly before her, and
jerked herself along as if she went by machinery, and her 芒聙聹Ow!芒聙聺 was
more suggestive of pins being run into her than of fear and anguish. Jo
gave a despairing groan, and Meg laughed outright, while Beth let her
bread burn as she watched the fun with interest. 芒聙聹It芒聙聶s no use! Do the
best you can when the time comes, and if the audience laughs, don芒聙聶t
blame me. Come on, Meg.芒聙聺

Then things went smoothly, for Don Pedro defied the world in a speech
of two pages without a single break. Hagar, the witch, chanted an awful
incantation over her kettleful of simmering toads, with weird effect.
Roderigo rent his chains asunder manfully, and Hugo died in agonies of
remorse and arsenic, with a wild, 芒聙聹Ha! Ha!芒聙聺

芒聙聹It芒聙聶s the best we芒聙聶ve had yet,芒聙聺 said Meg, as the dead villain sat up and
rubbed his elbows.

芒聙聹I don芒聙聶t see how you can write and act such splendid things, Jo. You芒聙聶re
a regular Shakespeare!芒聙聺 exclaimed Beth, who firmly believed that her
sisters were gifted with wonderful genius in all things.

芒聙聹Not quite,芒聙聺 replied Jo modestly. 芒聙聹I do think _The Witches Curse, an
Operatic Tragedy_ is rather a nice thing, but I芒聙聶d like to try
_Macbeth_, if we only had a trapdoor for Banquo. I always wanted to do

芒聙聹No, it芒聙聶s the toasting fork, with Mother芒聙聶s shoe on it instead of the

芒聙聹Glad to find you so merry, my girls,芒聙聺 said a cheery voice at the door,
and actors and audience turned to welcome a tall, motherly lady with a
芒聙聵can I help you芒聙聶 look about her which was truly delightful. She was not
elegantly dressed, but a noble-looking woman, and the girls thought the
gray cloak and unfashionable bonnet covered the most splendid mother in

芒聙聹Well, dearies, how have you got on today? There was so much to do,
getting the boxes ready to go tomorrow, that I didn芒聙聶t come home to
dinner. Has anyone called, Beth? How is your cold, Meg? Jo, you look
tired to death. Come and kiss me, baby.芒聙聺

While making these maternal inquiries Mrs. March got her wet things
off, her warm slippers on, and sitting down in the easy chair, drew Amy
to her lap, preparing to enjoy the happiest hour of her busy day. The
girls flew about, trying to make things comfortable, each in her own
way. Meg arranged the tea table, Jo brought wood and set chairs,
dropping, over-turning, and clattering everything she touched. Beth
trotted to and fro between parlor kitchen, quiet and busy, while Amy
gave directions to everyone, as she sat with her hands folded.

As they gathered about the table, Mrs. March said, with a particularly
happy face, 芒聙聹I芒聙聶ve got a treat for you after supper.芒聙聺

A quick, bright smile went round like a streak of sunshine. Beth
clapped her hands, regardless of the biscuit she held, and Jo tossed up
her napkin, crying, 芒聙聹A letter! A letter! Three cheers for Father!芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, a nice long letter. He is well, and thinks he shall get through
the cold season better than we feared. He sends all sorts of loving
wishes for Christmas, and an especial message to you girls,芒聙聺 said Mrs.
March, patting her pocket as if she had got a treasure there.

芒聙聹Hurry and get done! Don芒聙聶t stop to quirk your little finger and simper
over your plate, Amy,芒聙聺 cried Jo, choking on her tea and dropping her
bread, butter side down, on the carpet in her haste to get at the
treat.

Beth ate no more, but crept away to sit in her shadowy corner and brood
over the delight to come, till the others were ready.

芒聙聹I think it was so splendid in Father to go as chaplain when he was too
old to be drafted, and not strong enough for a soldier,芒聙聺 said Meg
warmly.

芒聙聹Don芒聙聶t I wish I could go as a drummer, a vivan芒聙聰what芒聙聶s its name? Or a
nurse, so I could be near him and help him,芒聙聺 exclaimed Jo, with a
groan.

芒聙聹It must be very disagreeable to sleep in a tent, and eat all sorts of
bad-tasting things, and drink out of a tin mug,芒聙聺 sighed Amy.

芒聙聹When will he come home, Marmee?芒聙聺 asked Beth, with a little quiver in
her voice.

芒聙聹Not for many months, dear, unless he is sick. He will stay and do his
work faithfully as long as he can, and we won芒聙聶t ask for him back a
minute sooner than he can be spared. Now come and hear the letter.芒聙聺

They all drew to the fire, Mother in the big chair with Beth at her
feet, Meg and Amy perched on either arm of the chair, and Jo leaning on
the back, where no one would see any sign of emotion if the letter
should happen to be touching. Very few letters were written in those
hard times that were not touching, especially those which fathers sent
home. In this one little was said of the hardships endured, the dangers
faced, or the homesickness conquered. It was a cheerful, hopeful
letter, full of lively descriptions of camp life, marches, and military
news, and only at the end did the writer芒聙聶s heart over-flow with
fatherly love and longing for the little girls at home.

芒聙聹Give them all of my dear love and a kiss. Tell them I think of them by
day, pray for them by night, and find my best comfort in their
affection at all times. A year seems very long to wait before I see
them, but remind them that while we wait we may all work, so that these
hard days need not be wasted. I know they will remember all I said to
them, that they will be loving children to you, will do their duty
faithfully, fight their bosom enemies bravely, and conquer themselves
so beautifully that when I come back to them I may be fonder and
prouder than ever of my little women.芒聙聺 Everybody sniffed when they came
to that part. Jo wasn芒聙聶t ashamed of the great tear that dropped off the
end of her nose, and Amy never minded the rumpling of her curls as she
hid her face on her mother芒聙聶s shoulder and sobbed out, 芒聙聹I am a selfish
girl! But I芒聙聶ll truly try to be better, so he mayn芒聙聶t be disappointed in
me by-and-by.芒聙聺

芒聙聹We all will,芒聙聺 cried Meg. 芒聙聹I think too much of my looks and hate to
work, but won芒聙聶t any more, if I can help it.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I芒聙聶ll try and be what he loves to call me, 芒聙聵a little woman芒聙聶 and not be
rough and wild, but do my duty here instead of wanting to be somewhere
else,芒聙聺 said Jo, thinking that keeping her temper at home was a much
harder task than facing a rebel or two down South.

Beth said nothing, but wiped away her tears with the blue army sock and
began to knit with all her might, losing no time in doing the duty that
lay nearest her, while she resolved in her quiet little soul to be all
that Father hoped to find her when the year brought round the happy
coming home.

Mrs. March broke the silence that followed Jo芒聙聶s words, by saying in her
cheery voice, 芒聙聹Do you remember how you used to play Pilgrims Progress
when you were little things? Nothing delighted you more than to have me
tie my piece bags on your backs for burdens, give you hats and sticks
and rolls of paper, and let you travel through the house from the
cellar, which was the City of Destruction, up, up, to the housetop,
where you had all the lovely things you could collect to make a
Celestial City.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What fun it was, especially going by the lions, fighting Apollyon, and
passing through the valley where the hob-goblins were,芒聙聺 said Jo.

芒聙聹I liked the place where the bundles fell off and tumbled downstairs,芒聙聺
said Meg.

芒聙聹I don芒聙聶t remember much about it, except that I was afraid of the cellar
and the dark entry, and always liked the cake and milk we had up at the
top. If I wasn芒聙聶t too old for such things, I芒聙聶d rather like to play it
over again,芒聙聺 said Amy, who began to talk of renouncing childish things
at the mature age of twelve.

芒聙聹We never are too old for this, my dear, because it is a play we are
playing all the time in one way or another. Our burdens are here, our
road is before us, and the longing for goodness and happiness is the
guide that leads us through many troubles and mistakes to the peace
which is a true Celestial City. Now, my little pilgrims, suppose you
begin again, not in play, but in earnest, and see how far on you can
get before Father comes home.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Really, Mother? Where are our bundles?芒聙聺 asked Amy, who was a very
literal young lady.

芒聙聹Each of you told what your burden was just now, except Beth. I rather
think she hasn芒聙聶t got any,芒聙聺 said her mother.

芒聙聹Yes, I have. Mine is dishes and dusters, and envying girls with nice
pianos, and being afraid of people.芒聙聺

Beth芒聙聶s bundle was such a funny one that everybody wanted to laugh, but
nobody did, for it would have hurt her feelings very much.

芒聙聹Let us do it,芒聙聺 said Meg thoughtfully. 芒聙聹It is only another name for
trying to be good, and the story may help us, for though we do want to
be good, it芒聙聶s hard work and we forget, and don芒聙聶t do our best.芒聙聺

芒聙聹We were in the Slough of Despond tonight, and Mother came and pulled
us out as Help did in the book. We ought to have our roll of
directions, like Christian. What shall we do about that?芒聙聺 asked Jo,
delighted with the fancy which lent a little romance to the very dull
task of doing her duty.

芒聙聹Look under your pillows Christmas morning, and you will find your
guidebook,芒聙聺 replied Mrs. March.

They talked over the new plan while old Hannah cleared the table, then
out came the four little work baskets, and the needles flew as the
girls made sheets for Aunt March. It was uninteresting sewing, but
tonight no one grumbled. They adopted Jo芒聙聶s plan of dividing the long
seams into four parts, and calling the quarters Europe, Asia, Africa,
and America, and in that way got on capitally, especially when they
talked about the different countries as they stitched their way through
them.

At nine they stopped work, and sang, as usual, before they went to bed.
No one but Beth could get much music out of the old piano, but she had
a way of softly touching the yellow keys and making a pleasant
accompaniment to the simple songs they sang. Meg had a voice like a
flute, and she and her mother led the little choir. Amy chirped like a
cricket, and Jo wandered through the airs at her own sweet will, always
coming out at the wrong place with a croak or a quaver that spoiled the
most pensive tune. They had always done this from the time they could
lisp...

Crinkle, crinkle, 芒聙聶ittle 芒聙聶tar,


and it had become a household custom, for the mother was a born singer.
The first sound in the morning was her voice as she went about the
house singing like a lark, and the last sound at night was the same
cheery sound, for the girls never grew too old for that familiar
lullaby.




CHAPTER TWO
A MERRY CHRISTMAS


Jo was the first to wake in the gray dawn of Christmas morning. No
stockings hung at the fireplace, and for a moment she felt as much
disappointed as she did long ago, when her little sock fell down
because it was crammed so full of goodies. Then she remembered her
mother芒聙聶s promise and, slipping her hand under her pillow, drew out a
little crimson-covered book. She knew it very well, for it was that
beautiful old story of the best life ever lived, and Jo felt that it
was a true guidebook for any pilgrim going on a long journey. She woke
Meg with a 芒聙聹Merry Christmas,芒聙聺 and bade her see what was under her
pillow. A green-covered book appeared, with the same picture inside,
and a few words written by their mother, which made their one present
very precious in their eyes. Presently Beth and Amy woke to rummage and
find their little books also, one dove-colored, the other blue, and all
sat looking at and talking about them, while the east grew rosy with
the coming day.

In spite of her small vanities, Margaret had a sweet and pious nature,
which unconsciously influenced her sisters, especially Jo, who loved
her very tenderly, and obeyed her because her advice was so gently
given.

芒聙聹Girls,芒聙聺 said Meg seriously, looking from the tumbled head beside her
to the two little night-capped ones in the room beyond, 芒聙聹Mother wants
us to read and love and mind these books, and we must begin at once. We
used to be faithful about it, but since Father went away and all this
war trouble unsettled us, we have neglected many things. You can do as
you please, but I shall keep my book on the table here and read a
little every morning as soon as I wake, for I know it will do me good
and help me through the day.芒聙聺

Then she opened her new book and began to read. Jo put her arm round
her and, leaning cheek to cheek, read also, with the quiet expression
so seldom seen on her restless face.

芒聙聹How good Meg is! Come, Amy, let芒聙聶s do as they do. I芒聙聶ll help you with
the hard words, and they芒聙聶ll explain things if we don芒聙聶t understand,芒聙聺
whispered Beth, very much impressed by the pretty books and her
sisters芒聙聶 example.

芒聙聹I芒聙聶m glad mine is blue,芒聙聺 said Amy. and then the rooms were very still
while the pages were softly turned, and the winter sunshine crept in to
touch the bright heads and serious faces with a Christmas greeting.

芒聙聹Where is Mother?芒聙聺 asked Meg, as she and Jo ran down to thank her for
their gifts, half an hour later.

芒聙聹Goodness only knows. Some poor creeter came a-beggin芒聙聶, and your ma
went straight off to see what was needed. There never was such a woman
for givin芒聙聶 away vittles and drink, clothes and firin芒聙聶,芒聙聺 replied Hannah,
who had lived with the family since Meg was born, and was considered by
them all more as a friend than a servant.

芒聙聹She will be back soon, I think, so fry your cakes, and have everything
ready,芒聙聺 said Meg, looking over the presents which were collected in a
basket and kept under the sofa, ready to be produced at the proper
time. 芒聙聹Why, where is Amy芒聙聶s bottle of cologne?芒聙聺 she added, as the little
flask did not appear.

芒聙聹She took it out a minute ago, and went off with it to put a ribbon on
it, or some such notion,芒聙聺 replied Jo, dancing about the room to take
the first stiffness off the new army slippers.

芒聙聹How nice my handkerchiefs look, don芒聙聶t they? Hannah washed and ironed
them for me, and I marked them all myself,芒聙聺 said Beth, looking proudly
at the somewhat uneven letters which had cost her such labor.

芒聙聹Bless the child! She芒聙聶s gone and put 芒聙聵Mother芒聙聶 on them instead of 芒聙聵M.
March芒聙聶. How funny!芒聙聺 cried Jo, taking one up.

芒聙聹Isn芒聙聶t that right? I thought it was better to do it so, because Meg芒聙聶s
initials are M.M., and I don芒聙聶t want anyone to use these but Marmee,芒聙聺
said Beth, looking troubled.

芒聙聹It芒聙聶s all right, dear, and a very pretty idea, quite sensible too, for
no one can ever mistake now. It will please her very much, I know,芒聙聺
said Meg, with a frown for Jo and a smile for Beth.

芒聙聹There芒聙聶s Mother. Hide the basket, quick!芒聙聺 cried Jo, as a door slammed
and steps sounded in the hall.

Amy came in hastily, and looked rather abashed when she saw her sisters
all waiting for her.

芒聙聹Where have you been, and what are you hiding behind you?芒聙聺 asked Meg,
surprised to see, by her hood and cloak, that lazy Amy had been out so
early.

芒聙聹Don芒聙聶t laugh at me, Jo! I didn芒聙聶t mean anyone should know till the time
came. I only meant to change the little bottle for a big one, and I
gave all my money to get it, and I芒聙聶m truly trying not to be selfish any
more.芒聙聺

As she spoke, Amy showed the handsome flask which replaced the cheap
one, and looked so earnest and humble in her little effort to forget
herself that Meg hugged her on the spot, and Jo pronounced her 芒聙聵a
trump芒聙聶, while Beth ran to the window, and picked her finest rose to
ornament the stately bottle.

芒聙聹You see I felt ashamed of my present, after reading and talking about
being good this morning, so I ran round the corner and changed it the
minute I was up, and I芒聙聶m so glad, for mine is the handsomest now.芒聙聺

Another bang of the street door sent the basket under the sofa, and the
girls to the table, eager for breakfast.

芒聙聹Merry Christmas, Marmee! Many of them! Thank you for our books. We
read some, and mean to every day,芒聙聺 they all cried in chorus.

芒聙聹Merry Christmas, little daughters! I芒聙聶m glad you began at once, and
hope you will keep on. But I want to say one word before we sit down.
Not far away from here lies a poor woman with a little newborn baby.
Six children are huddled into one bed to keep from freezing, for they
have no fire. There is nothing to eat over there, and the oldest boy
came to tell me they were suffering hunger and cold. My girls, will you
give them your breakfast as a Christmas present?芒聙聺

They were all unusually hungry, having waited nearly an hour, and for a
minute no one spoke, only a minute, for Jo exclaimed impetuously, 芒聙聹I芒聙聶m
so glad you came before we began!芒聙聺

芒聙聹May I go and help carry the things to the poor little children?芒聙聺 asked
Beth eagerly.

芒聙聹I shall take the cream and the muffings,芒聙聺 added Amy, heroically giving
up the article she most liked.

Meg was already covering the buckwheats, and piling the bread into one
big plate.

芒聙聹I thought you芒聙聶d do it,芒聙聺 said Mrs. March, smiling as if satisfied. 芒聙聹You
shall all go and help me, and when we come back we will have bread and
milk for breakfast, and make it up at dinnertime.芒聙聺

They were soon ready, and the procession set out. Fortunately it was
early, and they went through back streets, so few people saw them, and
no one laughed at the queer party.

A poor, bare, miserable room it was, with broken windows, no fire,
ragged bedclothes, a sick mother, wailing baby, and a group of pale,
hungry children cuddled under one old quilt, trying to keep warm.

How the big eyes stared and the blue lips smiled as the girls went in.

芒聙聹Ach, mein Gott! It is good angels come to us!芒聙聺 said the poor woman,
crying for joy.

芒聙聹Funny angels in hoods and mittens,芒聙聺 said Jo, and set them to laughing.

In a few minutes it really did seem as if kind spirits had been at work
there. Hannah, who had carried wood, made a fire, and stopped up the
broken panes with old hats and her own cloak. Mrs. March gave the
mother tea and gruel, and comforted her with promises of help, while
she dressed the little baby as tenderly as if it had been her own. The
girls meantime spread the table, set the children round the fire, and
fed them like so many hungry birds, laughing, talking, and trying to
understand the funny broken English.

芒聙聹Das ist gut!芒聙聺 芒聙聹Die Engel-kinder!芒聙聺 cried the poor things as they ate
and warmed their purple hands at the comfortable blaze. The girls had
never been called angel children before, and thought it very agreeable,
especially Jo, who had been considered a 芒聙聵Sancho芒聙聶 ever since she was
born. That was a very happy breakfast, though they didn芒聙聶t get any of
it. And when they went away, leaving comfort behind, I think there were
not in all the city four merrier people than the hungry little girls
who gave away their breakfasts and contented themselves with bread and
milk on Christmas morning.

芒聙聹That芒聙聶s loving our neighbor better than ourselves, and I like it,芒聙聺 said
Meg, as they set out their presents while their mother was upstairs
collecting clothes for the poor Hummels.

Not a very splendid show, but there was a great deal of love done up in
the few little bundles, and the tall vase of red roses, white
chrysanthemums, and trailing vines, which stood in the middle, gave
quite an elegant air to the table.

芒聙聹She芒聙聶s coming! Strike up, Beth! Open the door, Amy! Three cheers for
Marmee!芒聙聺 cried Jo, prancing about while Meg went to conduct Mother to
the seat of honor.

Beth played her gayest march, Amy threw open the door, and Meg enacted
escort with great dignity. Mrs. March was both surprised and touched,
and smiled with her eyes full as she examined her presents and read the
little notes which accompanied them. The slippers went on at once, a
new handkerchief was slipped into her pocket, well scented with Amy芒聙聶s
cologne, the rose was fastened in her bosom, and the nice gloves were
pronounced a perfect fit.

There was a good deal of laughing and kissing and explaining, in the
simple, loving fashion which makes these home festivals so pleasant at
the time, so sweet to remember long afterward, and then all fell to
work.

The morning charities and ceremonies took so much time that the rest of
the day was devoted to preparations for the evening festivities. Being
still too young to go often to the theater, and not rich enough to
afford any great outlay for private performances, the girls put their
wits to work, and necessity being the mother of invention, made
whatever they needed. Very clever were some of their productions,
pasteboard guitars, antique lamps made of old-fashioned butter boats
covered with silver paper, gorgeous robes of old cotton, glittering
with tin spangles from a pickle factory, and armor covered with the
same useful diamond shaped bits left in sheets when the lids of
preserve pots were cut out. The big chamber was the scene of many
innocent revels.

No gentleman were admitted, so Jo played male parts to her heart芒聙聶s
content and took immense satisfaction in a pair of russet leather boots
given her by a friend, who knew a lady who knew an actor. These boots,
an old foil, and a slashed doublet once used by an artist for some
picture, were Jo芒聙聶s chief treasures and appeared on all occasions. The
smallness of the company made it necessary for the two principal actors
to take several parts apiece, and they certainly deserved some credit
for the hard work they did in learning three or four different parts,
whisking in and out of various costumes, and managing the stage
besides. It was excellent drill for their memories, a harmless
amusement, and employed many hours which otherwise would have been
idle, lonely, or spent in less profitable society.

On Christmas night, a dozen girls piled onto the bed which was the
dress circle, and sat before the blue and yellow chintz curtains in a
most flattering state of expectancy. There was a good deal of rustling
and whispering behind the curtain, a trifle of lamp smoke, and an
occasional giggle from Amy, who was apt to get hysterical in the
excitement of the moment. Presently a bell sounded, the curtains flew
apart, and the _operatic tragedy_ began.

芒聙聹A gloomy wood,芒聙聺 according to the one playbill, was represented by a
few shrubs in pots, green baize on the floor, and a cave in the
distance. This cave was made with a clothes horse for a roof, bureaus
for walls, and in it was a small furnace in full blast, with a black
pot on it and an old witch bending over it. The stage was dark and the
glow of the furnace had a fine effect, especially as real steam issued
from the kettle when the witch took off the cover. A moment was allowed
for the first thrill to subside, then Hugo, the villain, stalked in
with a clanking sword at his side, a slouching hat, black beard,
mysterious cloak, and the boots. After pacing to and fro in much
agitation, he struck his forehead, and burst out in a wild strain,
singing of his hatred for Roderigo, his love for Zara, and his pleasing
resolution to kill the one and win the other. The gruff tones of Hugo芒聙聶s
voice, with an occasional shout when his feelings overcame him, were
very impressive, and the audience applauded the moment he paused for
breath. Bowing with the air of one accustomed to public praise, he
stole to the cavern and ordered Hagar to come forth with a commanding,
芒聙聹What ho, minion! I need thee!芒聙聺

Out came Meg, with gray horsehair hanging about her face, a red and
black robe, a staff, and cabalistic signs upon her cloak. Hugo demanded
a potion to make Zara adore him, and one to destroy Roderigo. Hagar, in
a fine dramatic melody, promised both, and proceeded to call up the
spirit who would bring the love philter.

Hither, hither, from thy home,
Airy sprite, I bid thee come!
Born of roses, fed on dew,
Charms and potions canst thou brew?
Bring me here, with elfin speed,
The fragrant philter which I need.
Make it sweet and swift and strong,
Spirit, answer now my song!


A soft strain of music sounded, and then at the back of the cave
appeared a little figure in cloudy white, with glittering wings, golden
hair, and a garland of roses on its head. Waving a wand, it sang...

Hither I come,
From my airy home,
Afar in the silver moon.
Take the magic spell,
And use it well,
Or its power will vanish soon!


And dropping a small, gilded bottle at the witch芒聙聶s feet, the spirit
vanished. Another chant from Hagar produced another apparition, not a
lovely one, for with a bang an ugly black imp appeared and, having
croaked a reply, tossed a dark bottle at Hugo and disappeared with a
mocking laugh. Having warbled his thanks and put the potions in his
boots, Hugo departed, and Hagar informed the audience that as he had
killed a few of her friends in times past, she had cursed him, and
intends to thwart his plans, and be revenged on him. Then the curtain
fell, and the audience reposed and ate candy while discussing the
merits of the play.

A good deal of hammering went on before the curtain rose again, but
when it became evident what a masterpiece of stage carpentery had been
got up, no one murmured at the delay. It was truly superb. A tower rose
to the ceiling, halfway up appeared a window with a lamp burning in it,
and behind the white curtain appeared Zara in a lovely blue and silver
dress, waiting for Roderigo. He came in gorgeous array, with plumed
cap, red cloak, chestnut lovelocks, a guitar, and the boots, of course.
Kneeling at the foot of the tower, he sang a serenade in melting tones.
Zara replied and, after a musical dialogue, consented to fly. Then came
the grand effect of the play. Roderigo produced a rope ladder, with
five steps to it, threw up one end, and invited Zara to descend.
Timidly she crept from her lattice, put her hand on Roderigo芒聙聶s
shoulder, and was about to leap gracefully down when 芒聙聹Alas! Alas for
Zara!芒聙聺 she forgot her train. It caught in the window, the tower
tottered, leaned forward, fell with a crash, and buried the unhappy
lovers in the ruins.

A universal shriek arose as the russet boots waved wildly from the
wreck and a golden head emerged, exclaiming, 芒聙聹I told you so! I told you
so!芒聙聺 With wonderful presence of mind, Don Pedro, the cruel sire, rushed
in, dragged out his daughter, with a hasty aside...

芒聙聹Don芒聙聶t laugh! Act as if it was all right!芒聙聺 and, ordering Roderigo up,
banished him from the kingdom with wrath and scorn. Though decidedly
shaken by the fall from the tower upon him, Roderigo defied the old
gentleman and refused to stir. This dauntless example fired Zara. She
also defied her sire, and he ordered them both to the deepest dungeons
of the castle. A stout little retainer came in with chains and led them
away, looking very much frightened and evidently forgetting the speech
he ought to have made.

Act third was the castle hall, and here Hagar appeared, having come to
free the lovers and finish Hugo. She hears him coming and hides, sees
him put the potions into two cups of wine and bid the timid little
servant, 芒聙聹Bear them to the captives in their cells, and tell them I
shall come anon.芒聙聺 The servant takes Hugo aside to tell him something,
and Hagar changes the cups for two others which are harmless.
Ferdinando, the 芒聙聵minion芒聙聶, carries them away, and Hagar puts back the
cup which holds the poison meant for Roderigo. Hugo, getting thirsty
after a long warble, drinks it, loses his wits, and after a good deal
of clutching and stamping, falls flat and dies, while Hagar informs him
what she has done in a song of exquisite power and melody.

This was a truly thrilling scene, though some persons might have
thought that the sudden tumbling down of a quantity of long red hair
rather marred the effect of the villain芒聙聶s death. He was called before
the curtain, and with great propriety appeared, leading Hagar, whose
singing was considered more wonderful than all the rest of the
performance put together.

Act fourth displayed the despairing Roderigo on the point of stabbing
himself because he has been told that Zara has deserted him. Just as
the dagger is at his heart, a lovely song is sung under his window,
informing him that Zara is true but in danger, and he can save her if
he will. A key is thrown in, which unlocks the door, and in a spasm of
rapture he tears off his chains and rushes away to find and rescue his
lady love.

Act fifth opened with a stormy scene between Zara and Don Pedro. He
wishes her to go into a convent, but she won芒聙聶t hear of it, and after a
touching appeal, is about to faint when Roderigo dashes in and demands
her hand. Don Pedro refuses, because he is not rich. They shout and
gesticulate tremendously but cannot agree, and Rodrigo is about to bear
away the exhausted Zara, when the timid servant enters with a letter
and a bag from Hagar, who has mysteriously disappeared. The latter
informs the party that she bequeaths untold wealth to the young pair
and an awful doom to Don Pedro, if he doesn芒聙聶t make them happy. The bag
is opened, and several quarts of tin money shower down upon the stage
till it is quite glorified with the glitter. This entirely softens the
stern sire. He consents without a murmur, all join in a joyful chorus,
and the curtain falls upon the lovers kneeling to receive Don Pedro芒聙聶s
blessing in attitudes of the most romantic grace.

Tumultuous applause followed but received an unexpected check, for the
cot bed, on which the dress circle was built, suddenly shut up and
extinguished the enthusiastic audience. Roderigo and Don Pedro flew to
the rescue, and all were taken out unhurt, though many were speechless
with laughter. The excitement had hardly subsided when Hannah appeared,
with 芒聙聹Mrs. March芒聙聶s compliments, and would the ladies walk down to
supper.芒聙聺

This was a surprise even to the actors, and when they saw the table,
they looked at one another in rapturous amazement. It was like Marmee
to get up a little treat for them, but anything so fine as this was
unheard of since the departed days of plenty. There was ice cream,
actually two dishes of it, pink and white, and cake and fruit and
distracting French bonbons and, in the middle of the table, four great
bouquets of hot house flowers.

It quite took their breath away, and they stared first at the table and
then at their mother, who looked as if she enjoyed it immensely.

芒聙聹Is it fairies?芒聙聺 asked Amy.

芒聙聹Santa Claus,芒聙聺 said Beth.

芒聙聹Mother did it.芒聙聺 And Meg smiled her sweetest, in spite of her gray
beard and white eyebrows.

芒聙聹Aunt March had a good fit and sent the supper,芒聙聺 cried Jo, with a
sudden inspiration.

芒聙聹All wrong. Old Mr. Laurence sent it,芒聙聺 replied Mrs. March.

芒聙聹The Laurence boy芒聙聶s grandfather! What in the world put such a thing
into his head? We don芒聙聶t know him!芒聙聺 exclaimed Meg.

芒聙聹Hannah told one of his servants about your breakfast party. He is an
odd old gentleman, but that pleased him. He knew my father years ago,
and he sent me a polite note this afternoon, saying he hoped I would
allow him to express his friendly feeling toward my children by sending
them a few trifles in honor of the day. I could not refuse, and so you
have a little feast at night to make up for the bread-and-milk
breakfast.芒聙聺

芒聙聹That boy put it into his head, I know he did! He芒聙聶s a capital fellow,
and I wish we could get acquainted. He looks as if he芒聙聶d like to know us
but he芒聙聶s bashful, and Meg is so prim she won芒聙聶t let me speak to him when
we pass,芒聙聺 said Jo, as the plates went round, and the ice began to melt
out of sight, with ohs and ahs of satisfaction.

芒聙聹You mean the people who live in the big house next door, don芒聙聶t you?芒聙聺
asked one of the girls. 芒聙聹My mother knows old Mr. Laurence, but says
he芒聙聶s very proud and doesn芒聙聶t like to mix with his neighbors. He keeps
his grandson shut up, when he isn芒聙聶t riding or walking with his tutor,
and makes him study very hard. We invited him to our party, but he
didn芒聙聶t come. Mother says he芒聙聶s very nice, though he never speaks to us

芒聙聹I like his manners, and he looks like a little gentleman, so I芒聙聶ve no

芒聙聹It芒聙聶s a mercy you didn芒聙聶t, Mother!芒聙聺 laughed Jo, looking at her boots.

芒聙聹I never had such a fine bouquet before! How pretty it is!芒聙聺 And Meg

芒聙聹They are lovely. But Beth芒聙聶s roses are sweeter to me,芒聙聺 said Mrs. March,

Beth nestled up to her, and whispered softly, 芒聙聹I wish I could send my

CHAPTER THREE
THE LAURENCE BOY


芒聙聹Jo! Jo! Where are you?芒聙聺 cried Meg at the foot of the garret stairs.

芒聙聹Here!芒聙聺 answered a husky voice from above, and, running up, Meg found

was Jo芒聙聶s favorite refuge, and here she loved to retire with half a

芒聙聹You must have gloves, or I won芒聙聶t go,芒聙聺 cried Meg decidedly. 芒聙聹Gloves are

芒聙聹You may have it, you may! Only don芒聙聶t stain it, and do behave nicely.

So Meg went away to 芒聙聵accept with thanks芒聙聶, look over her dress, and sing

芒聙聹Have a good time, dearies!芒聙聺 said Mrs. March, as the sisters went

芒聙聹No, winking isn芒聙聶t ladylike. I芒聙聶ll lift my eyebrows if any thing is

She could not roam about and amuse herself, for the burned breadth

things on.

Hannah scolded, Meg cried, and Jo was at her wits芒聙聶 end, till she
decided to take things into her own hands. Slipping out, she ran down
and, finding a servant, asked if he could get her a carriage. It
happened to be a hired waiter who knew nothing about the neighborhood

come for him, he said.

芒聙聹It芒聙聶s so early! You can芒聙聶t mean to go yet?芒聙聺 began Jo, looking relieved
but hesitating to accept the offer.

芒聙聹I always go early, I do, truly! Please let me take you home. It芒聙聶s all
on my way, you know, and it rains, they say.芒聙聺

That settled it, and telling him of Meg芒聙聶s mishap, Jo gratefully
accepted and rushed up to bring down the rest of the party. Hannah

girls talked over their party in freedom.

芒聙聹I had a capital time. Did you?芒聙聺 asked Jo, rumpling up her hair, and
making herself comfortable.

芒聙聹Yes, till I hurt myself. Sallie芒聙聶s friend, Annie Moffat, took a fancy

She is going in the spring when the opera comes, and it will be

芒聙聹I saw you dancing with the red headed man I ran away from. Was he

芒聙聹Oh, very! His hair is auburn, not red, and he was very polite, and I

芒聙聹He looked like a grasshopper in a fit when he did the new step. Laurie

芒聙聹No, but it was very rude. What were you about all that time, hidden

Jo told her adventures, and by the time she had finished they were at

芒聙聹Tell about the party! Tell about the party!芒聙聺

With what Meg called 芒聙聵a great want of manners芒聙聶 Jo had saved some

芒聙聹I declare, it really seems like being a fine young lady, to come home

芒聙聹I don芒聙聶t believe fine young ladies enjoy themselves a bit more than we

slippers that sprain our ankles when we are silly enough to wear them.芒聙聺
And I think Jo was quite right.




CHAPTER FOUR
BURDENS


芒聙聹Oh, dear, how hard it does seem to take up our packs and go on,芒聙聺

芒聙聹I wish it was Christmas or New Year芒聙聶s all the time. Wouldn芒聙聶t it be

芒聙聹We shouldn芒聙聶t enjoy ourselves half so much as we do now. But it does

This idea tickled Jo芒聙聶s fancy and put her in good spirits, but Meg

芒聙聹Where芒聙聶s the use of looking nice, when no one sees me but those cross

It芒聙聶s a shame!芒聙聺

Beth had a headache and lay on the sofa, trying to comfort herself with

芒聙聹Girls, girls, do be quiet one minute! I must get this off by the early

There was a momentary lull, broken by Hannah, who stalked in, laid two

Hannah never forgot to make them, no matter how busy or grumpy she

芒聙聹Cuddle your cats and get over your headache, Bethy. Goodbye, Marmee.
We are a set of rascals this morning, but we芒聙聶ll come home regular

They always looked back before turning the corner, for their mother was

Somehow it seemed as if they couldn芒聙聶t have got through the day without

芒聙聹If Marmee shook her fist instead of kissing her hand to us, it would

芒聙聹Don芒聙聶t use such dreadful expressions,芒聙聺 replied Meg from the depths of

芒聙聹I like good strong words that mean something,芒聙聺 replied Jo, catching

芒聙聹Call yourself any names you like, but I am neither a rascal nor a

芒聙聹You芒聙聶re a blighted being, and decidedly cross today because you can芒聙聶t

芒聙聹How ridiculous you are, Jo!芒聙聺 But Meg laughed at the nonsense and felt

芒聙聹Lucky for you I am, for if I put on crushed airs and tried to be

Jo gave her sister an encouraging pat on the shoulder as they parted

When Mr. March lost his property in trying to help an unfortunate
friend, the two oldest girls begged to be allowed to do something
toward their own support, at least. Believing that they could not begin
too early to cultivate energy, industry, and independence, their

which in spite of all obstacles is sure to succeed at last.

Margaret found a place as nursery governess and felt rich with her

trouble was poverty. She found it harder to bear than the others

bitter toward everyone sometimes, for she had not yet learned to know
how rich she was in the blessings which alone can make life happy.

Jo happened to suit Aunt March, who was lame and needed an active
person to wait upon her. The childless old lady had offered to adopt

the unworldly Marches only said...

芒聙聹We can芒聙聶t give up our girls for a dozen fortunes. Rich or poor, we will
keep together and be happy in one another.芒聙聺

The old lady wouldn芒聙聶t speak to them for a time, but happening to meet

I suspect that the real attraction was a large library of fine books,
which was left to dust and spiders since Uncle March died. Jo
remembered the kind old gentleman, who used to let her build railroads
and bridges with his big dictionaries, tell her stories about queer
pictures in his Latin books, and buy her cards of gingerbread whenever
he met her in the street. The dim, dusty room, with the busts staring
down from the tall bookcases, the cozy chairs, the globes, and best of
all, the wilderness of books in which she could wander where she liked,
made the library a region of bliss to her.

The moment Aunt March took her nap, or was busy with company, Jo
hurried to this quiet place, and curling herself up in the easy chair,
devoured poetry, romance, history, travels, and pictures like a regular
bookworm. But, like all happiness, it did not last long, for as sure as
she had just reached the heart of the story, the sweetest verse of a
song, or the most perilous adventure of her traveler, a shrill voice
called, 芒聙聹Josy-phine! Josy-phine!芒聙聺 and she had to leave her paradise to

Jo芒聙聶s ambition was to do something very splendid. What it was, she had

her greatest affliction in the fact that she couldn芒聙聶t read, run, and
ride as much as she liked. A quick temper, sharp tongue, and restless
spirit were always getting her into scrapes, and her life was a series

Beth was too bashful to go to school. It had been tried, but she

because Amy would have nothing old or ugly. Beth cherished them all the
more tenderly for that very reason, and set up a hospital for infirm
dolls. No pins were ever stuck into their cotton vitals, no harsh words
or blows were ever given them, no neglect ever saddened the heart of
the most repulsive, but all were fed and clothed, nursed and caressed
with an affection which never failed. One forlorn fragment of dollanity
had belonged to Jo and, having led a tempestuous life, was left a wreck

taken to her refuge. Having no top to its head, she tied on a neat
little cap, and as both arms and legs were gone, she hid these
deficiencies by folding it in a blanket and devoting her best bed to
this chronic invalid. If anyone had known the care lavished on that
dolly, I think it would have touched their hearts, even while they
laughed. She brought it bits of bouquets, she read to it, took it out

Beth had her troubles as well as the others, and not being an angel but

wouldn芒聙聶t keep in tune, when she was all alone. She sang like a little

There are many Beths in the world, shy and quiet, sitting in corners

If anybody had asked Amy what the greatest trial of her life was, she

芒聙聵Petrea芒聙聶s芒聙聶, it was only rather flat, and all the pinching in the world

芒聙聹Little Raphael,芒聙聺 as her sisters called her, had a decided talent for

Her little airs and graces were much admired, so were her

芒聙聹芒聙聵I don芒聙聶t understand what it芒聙聶s all about. Go back and begin it,

芒聙聹Back I went, and made the Primroses as interesting as ever I could.

芒聙聹She caught up her knitting, which had dropped out of her hands, gave

芒聙聹I saw something I liked this morning, and I meant to tell it at

order as she talked. 芒聙聹When I went to get some oysters for Hannah, Mr.

Mrs. March smiled and began at once, for she had told stories to this

芒聙聹Once upon a time, there were four girls, who had enough to eat and

CHAPTER FIVE
BEING NEIGHBORLY


芒聙聹What in the world are you going to do now, Jo?芒聙聺 asked Meg one snowy

芒聙聹Going out for exercise,芒聙聺 answered Jo with a mischievous twinkle in her

芒聙聹Never take advice! Can芒聙聶t keep still all day, and not being a pussycat,

Yet it seemed a lonely, lifeless sort of house, for no children

To Jo芒聙聶s lively fancy, this fine house seemed a kind of enchanted

CHAPTER SIX
BETH FINDS THE PALACE BEAUTIFUL


The big house did prove a Palace Beautiful, though it took some time
for all to get in, and Beth found it very hard to pass the lions. Old
Mr. Laurence was the biggest one, but after he had called, said
something funny or kind to each one of the girls, and talked over old
times with their mother, nobody felt much afraid of him, except timid
Beth. The other lion was the fact that they were poor and Laurie rich,
for this made them shy of accepting favors which they could not return.
But, after a while, they found that he considered them the benefactors,
and could not do enough to show how grateful he was for Mrs. March芒聙聶s
motherly welcome, their cheerful society, and the comfort he took in
that humble home of theirs. So they soon forgot their pride and
interchanged kindnesses without stopping to think which was the
greater.

All sorts of pleasant things happened about that time, for the new
friendship flourished like grass in spring. Every one liked Laurie, and
he privately informed his tutor that 芒聙聹the Marches were regularly

quick to feel the influences they brought about him, and their busy,
lively ways made him ashamed of the indolent life he led. He was tired

芒聙聹Never mind, let him take a holiday, and make it up afterward,芒聙聺 said

What good times they had, to be sure. Such plays and tableaux, such

Meg could walk in the conservatory whenever she liked and revel in

Beth had a rapture with her mother, and then rushed up to impart the

like the voice of a beloved friend.

She stayed till Hannah came to take her home to dinner, but she had no

After that, the little brown hood slipped through the hedge nearly

芒聙聹Mother, I芒聙聶m going to work Mr. Laurence a pair of slippers. He is so

can conquer pride. When she went home, he walked with her to her own

again, looking very stately and erect, like a handsome, soldierly old
gentleman, as he was.

When the girls saw that performance, Jo began to dance a jig, by way of

surprise, and Meg exclaimed, with up-lifted hands, 芒聙聹Well, I do believe
the world is coming to an end.芒聙聺

AMY芒聙聶S VALLEY OF HUMILIATION

Now Mr. Davis had declared limes a contraband article, and solemnly
vowed to publicly ferrule the first person who was found breaking the
law. This much-enduring man had succeeded in banishing chewing gum
after a long and stormy war, had made a bonfire of the confiscated
novels and newspapers, had suppressed a private post office, had
forbidden distortions of the face, nicknames, and caricatures, and done
all that one man could do to keep half a hundred rebellious girls in
order. Boys are trying enough to human patience, goodness knows, but
girls are infinitely more so, especially to nervous gentlemen with
tyrannical tempers and no more talent for teaching than Dr. Blimber.
Mr. Davis knew any quantity of Greek, Latin, algebra, and ologies of
all sorts so he was called a fine teacher, and manners, morals,
feelings, and examples were not considered of any particular
importance. It was a most unfortunate moment for denouncing Amy, and
Jenny knew it. Mr. Davis had evidently taken his coffee too strong that
morning, there was an east wind, which always affected his neuralgia,

During the fifteen minutes that followed, the proud and sensitive

herself, and with a parting box on her sister芒聙聶s ear, she rushed out of

alone.

The storm cleared up below, for Mrs. March came home, and, having heard
the story, soon brought Amy to a sense of the wrong she had done her
sister. Jo芒聙聶s book was the pride of her heart, and was regarded by her
family as a literary sprout of great promise. It was only half a dozen
little fairy tales, but Jo had worked over them patiently, putting her
whole heart into her work, hoping to make something good enough to
print. She had just copied them with great care, and had destroyed the
old manuscript, so that Amy芒聙聶s bonfire had consumed the loving work of
several years. It seemed a small loss to others, but to Jo it was a
dreadful calamity, and she felt that it never could be made up to her.
Beth mourned as for a departed kitten, and Meg refused to defend her
pet. Mrs. March looked grave and grieved, and Amy felt that no one
would love her till she had asked pardon for the act which she now
regretted more than any of them.

When the tea bell rang, Jo appeared, looking so grim and unapproachable
that it took all Amy芒聙聶s courage to say meekly...

芒聙聹Please forgive me, Jo. I芒聙聶m very, very sorry.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I never shall forgive you,芒聙聺 was Jo芒聙聶s stern answer, and from that
moment she ignored Amy entirely.

No one spoke of the great trouble, not even Mrs. March, for all had
learned by experience that when Jo was in that mood words were wasted,
and the wisest course was to wait till some little accident, or her own
generous nature, softened Jo芒聙聶s resentment and healed the breach. It was
not a happy evening, for though they sewed as usual, while their mother
read aloud from Bremer, Scott, or Edgeworth, something was wanting, and
the sweet home peace was disturbed. They felt this most when singing
time came, for Beth could only play, Jo stood dumb as a stone, and Amy
broke down, so Meg and Mother sang alone. But in spite of their efforts
to be as cheery as larks, the flutelike voices did not seem to chord as
well as usual, and all felt out of tune.

As Jo received her good-night kiss, Mrs. March whispered gently, 芒聙聹My
dear, don芒聙聶t let the sun go down upon your anger. Forgive each other,
help each other, and begin again tomorrow.芒聙聺

Jo wanted to lay her head down on that motherly bosom, and cry her
grief and anger all away, but tears were an unmanly weakness, and she
felt so deeply injured that she really couldn芒聙聶t quite forgive yet. So
she winked hard, shook her head, and said gruffly because Amy was
listening, 芒聙聹It was an abominable thing, and she doesn芒聙聶t deserve to be
forgiven.芒聙聺

With that she marched off to bed, and there was no merry or
confidential gossip that night.

Amy was much offended that her overtures of peace had been repulsed,
and began to wish she had not humbled herself, to feel more injured
than ever, and to plume herself on her superior virtue in a way which
was particularly exasperating. Jo still looked like a thunder cloud,
and nothing went well all day. It was bitter cold in the morning, she
dropped her precious turnover in the gutter, Aunt March had an attack
of the fidgets, Meg was sensitive, Beth would look grieved and wistful
when she got home, and Amy kept making remarks about people who were
always talking about being good and yet wouldn芒聙聶t even try when other
people set them a virtuous example.

芒聙聹Everybody is so hateful, I芒聙聶ll ask Laurie to go skating. He is always
kind and jolly, and will put me to rights, I know,芒聙聺 said Jo to herself,
and off she went.

Amy heard the clash of skates, and looked out with an impatient
exclamation.

芒聙聹There! She promised I should go next time, for this is the last ice we
shall have. But it芒聙聶s no use to ask such a crosspatch to take me.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Don芒聙聶t say that. You were very naughty, and it is hard to forgive the
loss of her precious little book, but I think she might do it now, and
I guess she will, if you try her at the right minute,芒聙聺 said Meg. 芒聙聹Go
after them. Don芒聙聶t say anything till Jo has got good-natured with
Laurie, than take a quiet minute and just kiss her, or do some kind
thing, and I芒聙聶m sure she芒聙聶ll be friends again with all her heart.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I芒聙聶ll try,芒聙聺 said Amy, for the advice suited her, and after a flurry to

It was not far to the river, but both were ready before Amy reached
them. Jo saw her coming, and turned her back. Laurie did not see, for
he was carefully skating along the shore, sounding the ice, for a warm
spell had preceded the cold snap.

芒聙聹I芒聙聶ll go on to the first bend, and see if it芒聙聶s all right before we
begin to race,芒聙聺 Amy heard him say, as he shot away, looking like a
young Russian in his fur-trimmed coat and cap.

Jo heard Amy panting after her run, stamping her feet and blowing on
her fingers as she tried to put her skates on, but Jo never turned and

satisfaction in her sister芒聙聶s troubles. She had cherished her anger till
it grew strong and took possession of her, as evil thoughts and
feelings always do unless cast out at once. As Laurie turned the bend,
he shouted back...

芒聙聹Keep near the shore. It isn芒聙聶t safe in the middle.芒聙聺 Jo heard, but Amy
was struggling to her feet and did not catch a word. Jo glanced over
her shoulder, and the little demon she was harboring said in her ear...

芒聙聹No matter whether she heard or not, let her take care of herself.芒聙聺

Laurie had vanished round the bend, Jo was just at the turn, and Amy,
far behind, striking out toward the smoother ice in the middle of the
river. For a minute Jo stood still with a strange feeling in her heart,
then she resolved to go on, but something held and turned her round,
just in time to see Amy throw up her hands and go down, with a sudden

heart stand still with fear. She tried to call Laurie, but her voice
was gone. She tried to rush forward, but her feet seemed to have no
strength in them, and for a second, she could only stand motionless,

out...

芒聙聹Bring a rail. Quick, quick!芒聙聺

How she did it, she never knew, but for the next few minutes she worked
as if possessed, blindly obeying Laurie, who was quite self-possessed,
and lying flat, held Amy up by his arm and hockey stick till Jo dragged
a rail from the fence, and together they got the child out, more
frightened than hurt.

芒聙聹Now then, we must walk her home as fast as we can. Pile our things on
her, while I get off these confounded skates,芒聙聺 cried Laurie, wrapping
his coat round Amy, and tugging away at the straps which never seemed
so intricate before.

Shivering, dripping, and crying, they got Amy home, and after an
exciting time of it, she fell asleep, rolled in blankets before a hot

pale and wild, with her things half off, her dress torn, and her hands
cut and bruised by ice and rails and refractory buckles. When Amy was

芒聙聹Are you sure she is safe?芒聙聺 whispered Jo, looking remorsefully at the

芒聙聹Quite safe, dear. She is not hurt, and won芒聙聶t even take cold, I think,
you were so sensible in covering and getting her home quickly,芒聙聺 replied
her mother cheerfully.

芒聙聹Laurie did it all. I only let her go. Mother, if she should die, it

heavy punishment which might have come upon her.

芒聙聹It芒聙聶s my dreadful temper! I try to cure it, I think I have, and then it

do?芒聙聺 cried poor Jo, in despair.

芒聙聹Watch and pray, dear, never get tired of trying, and never think it is
impossible to conquer your fault,芒聙聺 said Mrs. March, drawing the blowzy

芒聙聹You don芒聙聶t know, you can芒聙聶t guess how bad it is! It seems as if I could
do anything when I芒聙聶m in a passion. I get so savage, I could hurt anyone
and enjoy it. I芒聙聶m afraid I shall do something dreadful some day, and
spoil my life, and make everybody hate me. Oh, Mother, help me, do help

芒聙聹I will, my child, I will. Don芒聙聶t cry so bitterly, but remember this

yours, and it often takes us all our lives to conquer them. You think
your temper is the worst in the world, but mine used to be just like

芒聙聹Yours, Mother? Why, you are never angry!芒聙聺 And for the moment Jo forgot
remorse in surprise.

芒聙聹I芒聙聶ve been trying to cure it for forty years, and have only succeeded

The patience and the humility of the face she loved so well was a

knowledge that her mother had a fault like hers, and tried to mend it,

though forty years seemed rather a long time to watch and pray to a
girl of fifteen.

芒聙聹Mother, are you angry when you fold your lips tight together and go

芒聙聹Yes, I芒聙聶ve learned to check the hasty words that rise to my lips, and
when I feel that they mean to break out against my will, I just go away

and fastened up Jo芒聙聶s disheveled hair.

芒聙聹How did you learn to keep still? That is what troubles me, for the
sharp words fly out before I know what I芒聙聶m about, and the more I say
the worse I get, till it芒聙聶s a pleasure to hurt people芒聙聶s feelings and say
dreadful things. Tell me how you do it, Marmee dear.芒聙聺

芒聙聹My good mother used to help me...芒聙聺

芒聙聹As you do us...芒聙聺 interrupted Jo, with a grateful kiss.

芒聙聹But I lost her when I was a little older than you are, and for years

anyone else. I had a hard time, Jo, and shed a good many bitter tears

good. But by-and-by, when I had four little daughters round me and we

芒聙聹Poor Mother! What helped you then?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Your father, Jo. He never loses patience, never doubts or complains,
but always hopes, and works and waits so cheerfully that one is ashamed

that I must try to practice all the virtues I would have my little
girls possess, for I was their example. It was easier to try for your

when I spoke sharply rebuked me more than any words could have done,
and the love, respect, and confidence of my children was the sweetest
reward I could receive for my efforts to be the woman I would have them

芒聙聹Oh, Mother, if I芒聙聶m ever half as good as you, I shall be satisfied,芒聙聺

芒聙聹I hope you will be a great deal better, dear, but you must keep watch

芒聙聹I will try, Mother, I truly will. But you must help me, remind me, and

always folded your lips tight and went away. Was he reminding you

芒聙聹Yes. I asked him to help me so, and he never forgot it, but saved me
from many a sharp word by that little gesture and kind look.芒聙聺

Jo saw that her mother芒聙聶s eyes filled and her lips trembled as she
spoke, and fearing that she had said too much, she whispered anxiously,
芒聙聹Was it wrong to watch you and to speak of it? I didn芒聙聶t mean to be
rude, but it芒聙聶s so comfortable to say all I think to you, and feel so
safe and happy here.芒聙聺

芒聙聹My Jo, you may say anything to your mother, for it is my greatest
happiness and pride to feel that my girls confide in me and know how
much I love them.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I thought I芒聙聶d grieved you.芒聙聺

芒聙聹No, dear, but speaking of Father reminded me how much I miss him, how
much I owe him, and how faithfully I should watch and work to keep his
little daughters safe and good for him.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yet you told him to go, Mother, and didn芒聙聶t cry when he went, and never

芒聙聹I gave my best to the country I love, and kept my tears till he was

will surely be the happier for it in the end? If I don芒聙聶t seem to need
help, it is because I have a better friend, even than Father, to

life are beginning and may be many, but you can overcome and outlive
them all if you learn to feel the strength and tenderness of your
Heavenly Father as you do that of your earthly one. The more you love
and trust Him, the nearer you will feel to Him, and the less you will
depend on human power and wisdom. His love and care never tire or
change, can never be taken from you, but may become the source of
lifelong peace, happiness, and strength. Believe this heartily, and go
to God with all your little cares, and hopes, and sins, and sorrows, as
freely and confidingly as you come to your mother.芒聙聺

Jo芒聙聶s only answer was to hold her mother close, and in the silence which
followed the sincerest prayer she had ever prayed left her heart
without words. For in that sad yet happy hour, she had learned not only
the bitterness of remorse and despair, but the sweetness of self-denial
and self-control, and led by her mother芒聙聶s hand, she had drawn nearer to
the Friend who always welcomes every child with a love stronger than
that of any father, tenderer than that of any mother.

Amy stirred and sighed in her sleep, and as if eager to begin at once
to mend her fault, Jo looked up with an expression on her face which it
had never worn before.

芒聙聹I let the sun go down on my anger. I wouldn芒聙聶t forgive her, and today,
if it hadn芒聙聶t been for Laurie, it might have been too late! How could I
be so wicked?芒聙聺 said Jo, half aloud, as she leaned over her sister
softly stroking the wet hair scattered on the pillow.

As if she heard, Amy opened her eyes, and held out her arms, with a
smile that went straight to Jo芒聙聶s heart. Neither said a word, but they
hugged one another close, in spite of the blankets, and everything was
forgiven and forgotten in one hearty kiss.




CHAPTER NINE
MEG GOES TO VANITY FAIR


芒聙聹I do think it was the most fortunate thing in the world that those
children should have the measles just now,芒聙聺 said Meg, one April day, as
she stood packing the 芒聙聵go abroady芒聙聶 trunk in her room, surrounded by her
sisters.

芒聙聹And so nice of Annie Moffat not to forget her promise. A whole
fortnight of fun will be regularly splendid,芒聙聺 replied Jo, looking like
a windmill as she folded skirts with her long arms.

芒聙聹And such lovely weather, I芒聙聶m so glad of that,芒聙聺 added Beth, tidily
sorting neck and hair ribbons in her best box, lent for the great
occasion.

芒聙聹I wish I was going to have a fine time and wear all these nice
things,芒聙聺 said Amy with her mouth full of pins, as she artistically
replenished her sister芒聙聶s cushion.

芒聙聹I wish you were all going, but as you can芒聙聶t, I shall keep my
adventures to tell you when I come back. I芒聙聶m sure it芒聙聶s the least I can
do when you have been so kind, lending me things and helping me get
ready,芒聙聺 said Meg, glancing round the room at the very simple outfit,
which seemed nearly perfect in their eyes.

芒聙聹What did Mother give you out of the treasure box?芒聙聺 asked Amy, who had
not been present at the opening of a certain cedar chest in which Mrs.
March kept a few relics of past splendor, as gifts for her girls when
the proper time came.

芒聙聹A pair of silk stockings, that pretty carved fan, and a lovely blue
sash. I wanted the violet silk, but there isn芒聙聶t time to make it over,
so I must be contented with my old tarlaton.芒聙聺


芒聙聹It will look nice over my new muslin skirt, and the sash will set it
off beautifully. I wish I hadn芒聙聶t smashed my coral bracelet, for you
might have had it,芒聙聺 said Jo, who loved to give and lend, but whose
possessions were usually too dilapidated to be of much use.

芒聙聹There is a lovely old-fashioned pearl set in the treasure chest, but
Mother said real flowers were the prettiest ornament for a young girl,
and Laurie promised to send me all I want,芒聙聺 replied Meg. 芒聙聹Now, let me
see, there芒聙聶s my new gray walking suit, just curl up the feather in my
hat, Beth, then my poplin for Sunday and the small party, it looks
heavy for spring, doesn芒聙聶t it? The violet silk would be so nice. Oh,
dear!芒聙聺

芒聙聹Never mind, you芒聙聶ve got the tarlaton for the big party, and you always
look like an angel in white,芒聙聺 said Amy, brooding over the little store
of finery in which her soul delighted.

芒聙聹It isn芒聙聶t low-necked, and it doesn芒聙聶t sweep enough, but it will have to
do. My blue housedress looks so well, turned and freshly trimmed, that
I feel as if I芒聙聶d got a new one. My silk sacque isn芒聙聶t a bit the fashion,
and my bonnet doesn芒聙聶t look like Sallie芒聙聶s. I didn芒聙聶t like to say
anything, but I was sadly disappointed in my umbrella. I told Mother
black with a white handle, but she forgot and bought a green one with a

I know I shall feel ashamed of it beside Annie芒聙聶s silk one with a gold

芒聙聹Change it,芒聙聺 advised Jo.

芒聙聹I won芒聙聶t be so silly, or hurt Marmee芒聙聶s feelings, when she took so much
pains to get my things. It芒聙聶s a nonsensical notion of mine, and I芒聙聶m not
going to give up to it. My silk stockings and two pairs of new gloves

sort of elegant, with two new pairs, and the old ones cleaned up for
common.芒聙聺 And Meg took a refreshing peep at her glove box.

芒聙聹Annie Moffat has blue and pink bows on her nightcaps. Would you put
some on mine?芒聙聺 she asked, as Beth brought up a pile of snowy muslins,
fresh from Hannah芒聙聶s hands.

芒聙聹No, I wouldn芒聙聶t, for the smart caps won芒聙聶t match the plain gowns without
any trimming on them. Poor folks shouldn芒聙聶t rig,芒聙聺 said Jo decidedly.

芒聙聹I wonder if I shall ever be happy enough to have real lace on my
clothes and bows on my caps?芒聙聺 said Meg impatiently.

芒聙聹You said the other day that you芒聙聶d be perfectly happy if you could only
go to Annie Moffat芒聙聶s,芒聙聺 observed Beth in her quiet way.

芒聙聹So I did! Well, I am happy, and I won芒聙聶t fret, but it does seem as if
the more one gets the more one wants, doesn芒聙聶t it? There now, the trays
are ready, and everything in but my ball dress, which I shall leave for
Mother to pack,芒聙聺 said Meg, cheering up, as she glanced from the
half-filled trunk to the many times pressed and mended white tarlaton,
which she called her 芒聙聵ball dress芒聙聶 with an important air.

The next day was fine, and Meg departed in style for a fortnight of
novelty and pleasure. Mrs. March had consented to the visit rather
reluctantly, fearing that Margaret would come back more discontented
than she went. But she begged so hard, and Sallie had promised to take
good care of her, and a little pleasure seemed so delightful after a
winter of irksome work that the mother yielded, and the daughter went
to take her first taste of fashionable life.

The Moffats were very fashionable, and simple Meg was rather daunted,
at first, by the splendor of the house and the elegance of its
occupants. But they were kindly people, in spite of the frivolous life
they led, and soon put their guest at her ease. Perhaps Meg felt,
without understanding why, that they were not particularly cultivated
or intelligent people, and that all their gilding could not quite
conceal the ordinary material of which they were made. It certainly was
agreeable to fare sumptuously, drive in a fine carriage, wear her best
frock every day, and do nothing but enjoy herself. It suited her
exactly, and soon she began to imitate the manners and conversation of
those about her, to put on little airs and graces, use French phrases,
crimp her hair, take in her dresses, and talk about the fashions as
well as she could. The more she saw of Annie Moffat芒聙聶s pretty things,
the more she envied her and sighed to be rich. Home now looked bare and
dismal as she thought of it, work grew harder than ever, and she felt
that she was a very destitute and much-injured girl, in spite of the
new gloves and silk stockings.

She had not much time for repining, however, for the three young girls
were busily employed in 芒聙聵having a good time芒聙聶. They shopped, walked,
rode, and called all day, went to theaters and operas or frolicked at
home in the evening, for Annie had many friends and knew how to
entertain them. Her older sisters were very fine young ladies, and one
was engaged, which was extremely interesting and romantic, Meg thought.
Mr. Moffat was a fat, jolly old gentleman, who knew her father, and
Mrs. Moffat, a fat, jolly old lady, who took as great a fancy to Meg as
her daughter had done. Everyone petted her, and 芒聙聵Daisey芒聙聶, as they
called her, was in a fair way to have her head turned.

When the evening for the small party came, she found that the poplin
wouldn芒聙聶t do at all, for the other girls were putting on thin dresses
and making themselves very fine indeed. So out came the tarlatan,
looking older, limper, and shabbier than ever beside Sallie芒聙聶s crisp new
one. Meg saw the girls glance at it and then at one another, and her
cheeks began to burn, for with all her gentleness she was very proud.
No one said a word about it, but Sallie offered to dress her hair, and
Annie to tie her sash, and Belle, the engaged sister, praised her white
arms. But in their kindness Meg saw only pity for her poverty, and her
heart felt very heavy as she stood by herself, while the others
laughed, chattered, and flew about like gauzy butterflies. The hard,
bitter feeling was getting pretty bad, when the maid brought in a box
of flowers. Before she could speak, Annie had the cover off, and all
were exclaiming at the lovely roses, heath, and fern within.

芒聙聹It芒聙聶s for Belle, of course, George always sends her some, but these are
altogether ravishing,芒聙聺 cried Annie, with a great sniff.

芒聙聹They are for Miss March, the man said. And here芒聙聶s a note,芒聙聺 put in the

芒聙聹Well, if that isn芒聙聶t the greatest rubbish I ever heard,芒聙聺 cried Jo
